question_answered: What makes one learning card narrow enough to stay coherent while still useful?
one_sentence_definition: A single-card boundary is the rule that one card should answer one dominant question and stop before adjacent questions take over.
essence: It compresses a thread to one retrieval unit without flattening the idea into something too vague.
why_it_matters: It prevents one card from becoming a mixed concept-method-mechanism bundle.
It makes graph placement and later upgrades easier.
core_logic: Identify the dominant question in the thread.
Keep only the evidence that serves that question.
Route leftover material to another card instead of stretching the current one.
prerequisites: The card family already has clear type boundaries.
Creating a second card is cheaper than overloading the first card.
failure_boundaries: It fails when the dominant question is still unresolved.
It also fails when the user really needs a comparison card rather than a single-node card.
confusions: It is easy to confuse a single-card boundary with oversimplification; the real target is coherence, not forced thinness.
examples: A thread that mainly defines what a boundary is becomes one concept card, while its trimming procedure becomes a separate method card.
counter_examples: A card that explains what boundary means, why drift happens, how to fix it, and which misconception to avoid is already too wide.
common_misunderstandings: One card does not mean one paragraph; it means one dominant question.
needs_validation: Where should borderline comparison-heavy threads split into two cards instead of one?

parent_concepts: [[Knowledge Unit Coherence]] | Single-card boundary is one concrete rule for maintaining coherent learning units.
child_concepts: [[Section Priority Compression]] | Compression is one downstream way to enforce the boundary.
adjacent_concepts: [[Atomic Notes]] | Both seek compactness, but this card is tuned for learning-card routing rather than generic note size.

prerequisites: [[Card Type Routing]] | Boundary quality depends on choosing the right card type first.
enables: [[Section Priority Compression]] | A stable boundary makes section trimming much easier.
contrasts: [[Full Thread Capture]] | One tries to preserve everything, while this concept defends selective inclusion.
corrections: [[More Sections Make a Better Learning Card]] | This concept corrects the pressure to keep adding sections just because space exists.

direct_routes: When the real issue is how to enforce the boundary section by section, route to [[Section Priority Compression]].
When the issue is why boundaries keep widening by default, route to [[Section Bloat Drift]].
secondary_routes: If [[Section Priority Compression]] trims the card but drift still returns, continue to [[More Sections Make a Better Learning Card]] to correct the underlying belief.
gap_signals: If the graph has boundary and trimming cards but no card about choosing between split versus merge decisions, add a new Method card for split-merge arbitration.
stop_rules: Stop staying in this concept once the user agrees on the boundary and the next question becomes operational trimming.

current_status_notes: This concept already behaves like a reliable local boundary anchor for the card family.
next_goal: Add a stronger split-versus-merge rule for borderline cases.
growing_checklist: Explain why boundary discipline is not the same as oversimplification.
stable_checklist: Show at least three strong relationships.
expert_ready_checklist: Add one explicit gap signal and one stop rule.

main_reasons: The card already works as a stable local node.
It has early routing value inside the learning-card family.
missing_evidence: It still lacks stronger arbitration when a thread could reasonably split into two cards.
next_rules: If a thread has two equally dominant questions, split before writing either card.

current_upgrade_tasks: Capture one real thread where split-versus-merge was hard to decide.
